responses: I currently live in Rock Springs - for the past 34 years. Prior to that I lived in Green River. I am a lifelong resident of Sweetwater County, Wyoming

I worked 20 years for Church & Dwight Company (Arm & Hammer Baking Soda) in various positions. For 17 years I worked shiftwork and served as the Maintenance Supervisor when I left to join the Fire Service full time. I worked rotating shifts for 17 years and drove 80 miles round-trip to work every day. 
I have been a full-time Fire Chief for 20 years - 9 years with Sweetwater County Fire District 1 and 11 years for the Rock Springs Fire Department. I currently serve as the Chair of the Rock Springs-Sweetwater County Airport Board (13 years, 11 as Chair), Vice-Chair of the Sweetwater Combined Communications Board (16 years, 5 as Chair), and Chairman of the Wyoming State Emergency Response Committee.
I am the 1st Vice President of the Wyoming Fire Chief's Association, and the Missouri Valley Division Director to the International Association of Fire Chiefs representing the states of Colorado, Nebraska, North Dakota, South Dakota, Iowa, Kansas, Missouri, and Wyoming.

Ensuring that we have our fair share of water tops the list - it is literally our life force. Access to mental health resources and suicide prevention - we have lost enough of our own. Promote economic development - let the world know that Wyoming is open for business. Ensure an effective education for every child in Wyoming - meet the student where they are regarding CTE, post-secondary education, etc. Wyoming needs affordable housing to accommodate the workforce needed to promote growth.
Explore and expand options for Mobile-Integrated healthcare and Community Paramedicine. Bring healthcare to where the people are.

I do not support the sale or transfer of public lands in Wyoming except in very narrow circumstances - only parcels that abut municipal corporate limits and then only for affordable housing. I would lean heavily on our US Congressional Delegation to keep our lands and access intact with multi-purpose use in the forefront.
Property tax is the tax closest to the people and is the tax which most supports local government. These funds should be managed with objective restraint and discretion.

We can't expect people to invest in Wyoming if we do not invest in ourselves. Responsible investment in infrastructure such as water, waste water, power transmission, and roads will attract investment from outside. At the local level, communities also need to invest in themselves to create and improve quality of life.
I am not well informed enough to answer this question.
Rock Springs, since 2021.  In Wyoming since 2019.  Previously lived in Cody, WY 2001-2004

I am an Army Special Operations veteran and a military-trained Physician Associate specializing in Orthopedics. For most of my adult life, I have served as either a protector or a healer.

My wife Tracy and I have been married for nearly three decades. We have four children and three grandchildren. We moved to Rock Springs in 2021 and previously lived in Cody from 2001-2004 and 2019-2021.

I currently serve in the Wyoming House of Representatives on the Labor, Health and Social Services Committee and the Transportation, Highways and Military Affairs Committee.

Before serving in the Legislature, I spent years caring for patients and helping families through difficult medical decisions. In the Legislature, I have focused on protecting constitutional rights, supporting parents, improving healthcare access, promoting fiscal responsibility and ensuring government remains accountable to the people.

I am running for reelection because Wyoming is worth protecting.

Wyoming's biggest challenge is preserving our way of life while keeping the state affordable for working families, retirees and young people. Rising property taxes, government spending, healthcare costs and workforce shortages are concerns I hear regularly from constituents.

Our greatest opportunities remain the industries and people that built Wyoming: energy, agriculture, small business, natural resources and a strong culture of independence.

As a legislator, I will continue working to reduce the tax burden on Wyoming families, protect private property rights, support reliable and affordable energy production, improve healthcare access and reduce unnecessary regulations.

Wyoming does not have a revenue problem; it has a spending and prioritization problem. Government should focus on its core responsibilities, live within its means and respect the taxpayers who fund it.

As a healthcare provider, I have seen firsthand the challenges facing patients, providers and rural hospitals.

The Legislature cannot solve every problem in healthcare, but it can remove barriers that drive up costs and reduce access. We should continue expanding telehealth where appropriate, encourage more healthcare professionals to practice in Wyoming and reduce unnecessary regulations that make care more expensive.

We should also increase price transparency so patients know what they are paying for and can make informed decisions about their care.

Many rural communities struggle to attract providers. We should look for practical ways to support healthcare delivery in those areas while maintaining quality care close to home.

Healthcare decisions should be made by patients and their providers, not bureaucracies. My goal is simple: lower costs, increase access and ensure Wyoming families can receive quality care when and where they need it.

I do not support the sale of Wyoming's public lands and would vote against legislation that broadly authorizes their sale.

Public lands are important to Wyoming's economy, culture and way of life. Hunters, anglers, ranchers, energy producers, outdoor recreationists and local communities all rely on access to these lands.

At the same time, I believe Wyoming should have a stronger voice in how federal lands within our borders are managed. Decisions made in Washington, D.C., often fail to account for the realities faced by the people who live and work here.

Land management should follow multiple-use principles that balance recreation, grazing, wildlife, energy production and responsible stewardship.

The people closest to the land generally understand it best. Wyoming's interests are best served when local communities have a meaningful role in land management decisions.

I strongly support meaningful property tax relief and ultimately believe Wyoming should work toward eliminating property taxes on primary residences.

People should not spend a lifetime paying off a home only to face the possibility of being taxed out of it. Property ownership is one of the most basic rights we have.

At the same time, counties, municipalities and special districts provide services that citizens depend upon. The answer is not to continue raising taxes. The answer is to prioritize spending and focus government on its core responsibilities.

Wyoming has significant financial resources and reserve accounts. Before asking taxpayers for more, government should carefully examine where money is being spent and whether those expenditures are truly necessary.

I believe Wyoming can provide meaningful property tax relief while maintaining essential services through responsible budgeting, spending discipline and long-term planning.

The Legislature's primary role in economic development is to remove obstacles, not create new programs.

Government does not create prosperity. Wyoming workers, ranchers, energy producers, small business owners and entrepreneurs do.

The Legislature should maintain a stable regulatory environment, protect private property rights, keep taxes low, support reliable infrastructure and ensure businesses have the freedom to grow and hire.

I do not believe government should be in the business of picking winners and losers. When government starts favoring certain industries or businesses, someone else ends up paying the bill.

The best economic development policy is simple: lower taxes, fewer unnecessary regulations, affordable energy and a government that stays focused on its proper role.

I have chosen not to accept direct campaign contributions from PACs or professional associations. My campaign support has come from individual citizens who believe in the principles and policies I advocate.

That is a personal decision, and other candidates are free to make different choices within the law.

Transparency is the most important part of any campaign finance system. Voters should be able to easily see who is contributing to campaigns and how campaign funds are being spent.

Wyoming's reporting requirements already provide substantial public disclosure. If reforms are considered, they should focus on improving transparency and public access to information without creating unnecessary burdens that discourage citizens from running for office.

Ultimately, informed voters are the best safeguard for accountable government.

I am the 1st Vice President of the Wyoming Fire Chief's Association, and the Missouri Valley Division Director to the International Association of Fire Chiefs representing the states of Colorado, Nebraska, North Dakota, South Dakota, Iowa, Kansas, Missouri, and Wyoming.

I am not well informed enough to answer this question.

questions: Where do you live currently? How long have you lived there? How long have you lived in Wyoming? Where were you born?
What age will you be on Election Day, Nov. 3, 2026?
Please tell the voters about yourself including your background and qualifications.
What are the biggest challenges and opportunities facing Wyoming today? As a legislator, how would you address them?

We asked WyoFile readers to rank issues that are important to them, and healthcare costs and access topped the list. What can the Legislature do to make healthcare more affordable and accessible to Wyomingites?
Do you support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming? How do you plan to ensure that state and federal land is managed in the best interest of the people of Wyoming?
No two Wyoming counties are the same when it comes to how they generate revenue. As a legislator, how would you approach the public's desire for property tax relief with local revenue needs?
What role, if any, should the Wyoming Legislature play in economic development?
Explain your values when it comes to campaign donations. Does Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures need reform?
pageProps.questionnaireIntro:
The answers below were solicited from candidates via a written questionnaire created by WyoFile reporters and editors in June 2026. Responses are presented exactly as submitted, without fact-checking, wordsmithing or editing for grammar, punctuation or spelling errors. 

Legislative candidates were invited to respond to the questionnaire several times by email and by phone. Out of fairness to the candidates who met the deadline, WyoFile will not add responses after the guide's publication.
pageProps.aboutProject:
WyoFile produced this guide with coding and web development by Thomas Musselman, editing by Tennessee Watson and production assistance by Calla Shosh. Contact Tennessee Watson with questions, corrections or suggestions at <tennessee@wyofile.com>.

WyoFile is a Wyoming 501(c)3 nonprofit, independent, member-supported, public-interest news service reporting on the people, places and policy of Wyoming. WyoFile's [donor base](https://wyofile.com/supporters/) is politically diverse, including some donors who are candidates in this year's election. We maintain separation between sources of revenue and editorial direction, decisions and oversight. Acceptance of financial support does not constitute implied or actual endorsement of donors or their products, services or opinions, and does not confer editorial influence, nor does donor support imply any endorsement of WyoFile's reporting, commentary or editorial position.

This material is available for republication by select media outlets. Contact Collaborations Editor Rebecca Huntington at <rebecca@wyofile.com> to learn how.
